driver.body: Couriers are the sell side of the logistics marketplace, and Kepler's thesis is that this side is not found, it is manufactured order by order. Couriers are **half the market and the more volatile half**, because their participation is priced by the hour against every other earning option in the city [S-039] — a rider who could drive for mobility, run an errand, or simply stay home re-prices their labor every hour, so supply that was online at 6pm is gone by 7 unless something on the screen holds it. Supply is therefore not recruited once; it is summoned, and that is why the courier-facing surface — the app in a rider's hand — is treated as a first-class strategic asset, not a UI afterthought [S-039]. The two mechanisms below are how the summoning is engineered: the job-offer screen that makes a single order legibly worth accepting in the seconds a courier gives it, and the elasticity model that prices the marginal hour so Kepler never subsidizes the whole population to solve a problem that lives only in the tail.

mechanisms.body:
A courier decides to **accept or decline in under four seconds** [S-040]. Everything that determines whether L — marketplace liquidity — holds has to be legible inside that window: the guaranteed payout, the honest ETA, the surge multiplier, and the batching. The live in-app job-offer screen and the push that precedes it are real production copy [S-308], and every element on it is a liquidity lever with a specific cross-feed behind it.

- The **€14.20 is guaranteed, not estimated** — the courier is paid that number even if the drop runs long. That guarantee is only underwritable because D4 (payments) pays out in nine seconds and D6 (the data platform) predicts route time tightly enough that guarantee variance stays at ±7% [S-040]. A standalone app that cannot bound its own route-time variance cannot promise a hard number without bleeding on every long drop.
- The "~22 min" and "5.4 km" are **honest because D1 mobility traces give congestion-real ETAs** [S-309]. A competitor quoting optimistic times trains its own couriers to distrust the app — which is precisely the 58%→39% funnel cliff reproduced in miniature at the level of a single offer [S-040].
- The "bike at 88%" line ties to **D2 (energy)**: the app only offers a battery-heavy multi-stop batch to a courier whose charge — on Kepler's network, at Kepler's cost — can actually complete it [S-040]. The batch that most improves liquidity is only ever shown to the courier who can finish it.
- The "acceptance 94% · keeps Priority tier" line is the **incentive spine**: acceptance rate is the courier's stake in the matching engine (Driver 3), surfaced to them at the exact moment the decision is made [S-040].

Read together, the screen is the flywheel compressed into four seconds. Four separate domains — D1, D2, D4, D6 — each pay into one accept/decline decision, and a single-vertical courier app cannot reproduce that offer because it touches none of those four surfaces at once.

component_data.title: New job offer · tap in 4s
lines: €14.20 guaranteed — paid even if the drop runs long
Your bike at 88% — batch fits this charge
component_data.meta: Push preview: "€14.20 guaranteed · 22 min · Accept to keep Priority tier" — real production copy [S-308]

mechanisms.body:
Kepler models the marginal cost of an online courier-hour by tier, and the table is **the supply curve made operational** — the same model that prices surge in Driver 4 [S-041]. It splits supply into two populations that behave nothing alike:

- The **professional core is inelastic and cheap** — it comes online regardless of small price moves and clears at low marginal cost.
- The **casual tail is elastic and expensive**, summoned online only when demand spikes past what the core can clear.

The strategy reads straight off the table [S-310]. Protect the core's effective pay at almost any cost, because losing 1% of them costs disproportionate volume; and use the casual tail's high elasticity as a supply valve — dial surge to pull it online for the roughly two hours a day it is actually needed, and let it recede otherwise. The failure mode designed against is **subsidizing the whole population to solve a problem that lives in the tail**, which is how standalone marketplaces burn cash into a thin market: they raise the price everyone sees to summon the few who were the constraint.

The resulting cost advantage is structural, not promotional. Because D2 lowers the core's real energy cost and D4 lowers their payout friction to zero, **Kepler's core clears at €9.10/hr where a competitor renting charging and paying weekly clears near €11.20** — a 19% supply-cost advantage that widens L at equal price [S-041]. The cross-feeds are not a story told after the fact; they show up as a fixed discount on the single largest variable cost in the marketplace.
